concepts.title: Attention Pooling over Variable-Sized Data
concepts.summary: Attention pooling can operate over databases of arbitrary size without changing the pooling procedure.

provenance.quotedText: Just as convenient is the fact that attention can operate on arbitrarily large databases without the need to change the way the attention pooling operation is performed.

lesson.plainTitle: Using Attention with Any Amount of Data

steps.text: Attention pooling can handle a database of any size. The same pooling process works whether the database has few items or many.

citation.quotedText: attention can operate on arbitrarily large databases without the need to change the way the attention pooling operation is performed.

analogies.cooking: Imagine using the same tasting method for three soup ingredients or thirty; the process stays the same even as the ingredient list grows.
analogies.sports: Like a coach reviewing a small squad or a huge roster with the same selection process, regardless of how many players are available.
analogies.music: Imagine a producer using the same mixing approach for a few tracks or an entire orchestra; only the number of inputs changes.
analogies.video-games: Like using the same inventory search whether your character holds five items or hundreds; the procedure still works across the whole collection.
analogies.cars: Imagine inspecting a small parking lot or a massive garage with the same method for finding and combining useful vehicle details.
analogies.gardening: Like surveying a tiny flower bed or a vast garden with the same process for gathering useful observations from every plant.

steps.text: It combines information from many key-value pairs, where keys help identify relevant items and values contain the information to combine.

citation.quotedText: The attention mechanism allows us to aggregate data from many (key, value) pairs.

analogies.cooking: Imagine recipe labels as keys pointing to ingredients, while each ingredient’s flavor is the value blended into the final dish.
analogies.sports: Like jersey numbers helping identify relevant players, while their skills and statistics provide the information combined into a game plan.
analogies.music: Imagine track names as keys that locate sounds, while the notes and rhythms are values blended into the final mix.
analogies.video-games: Like map markers identifying useful locations, while the loot or information found there supplies what gets combined for your next move.
analogies.cars: Imagine dashboard labels identifying relevant systems, while their readings provide the information combined to understand the car’s condition.
analogies.gardening: Like plant tags identifying which crops matter, while details such as growth and moisture provide the information combined into a garden plan.

concepts.title: Dot Product
concepts.summary: The dot product combines two vectors by summing products of elements at corresponding positions.

provenance.quotedText: Given two vectors , their dot product (also known as inner product, ) is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position: .

steps.text: A dot product takes two vectors—ordered lists of numbers—multiplies values in matching positions, and adds those products together.

citation.quotedText: their dot product (also known as inner product, ) is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position

analogies.cooking: Imagine pairing amounts from two recipe lists, multiplying each matched pair, then adding those products into one total.
analogies.sports: Like matching each player’s statistics with corresponding scoring factors, multiplying each pair, and adding the products for one score.
analogies.music: Imagine matching note values with volume levels, multiplying each corresponding pair, then adding the products into one result.
analogies.video-games: Like pairing character attributes with matching bonus multipliers, multiplying each pair, and adding everything into one final score.
analogies.cars: Imagine matching a car’s performance numbers with corresponding factors, multiplying each pair, then adding the products into one rating.
analogies.gardening: Like pairing quantities of garden supplies with matching per-unit values, multiplying each pair, then adding the products for one total.

steps.text: In practical terms, first multiply the two vectors element by element, then add all the results.

citation.quotedText: we can calculate the dot product of two vectors by performing an elementwise multiplication followed by a sum

analogies.cooking: Imagine multiplying each ingredient quantity by its matching cost, then adding every result to find the recipe’s total cost.
analogies.sports: Like multiplying each shot count by its matching point value, then adding the results to calculate the total score.
analogies.music: Imagine multiplying each instrument’s level by its matching mix setting, then adding all the results into one combined value.
analogies.video-games: Like multiplying each collected item count by its matching point value, then adding the results for your total score.
analogies.cars: Imagine multiplying each trip segment’s distance by its matching fuel-use figure, then adding all the results into one total.
analogies.gardening: Like multiplying each seed packet count by its matching price, then adding every result to find the total cost.

steps.text: A dot product can calculate a weighted sum, where each value is multiplied by a chosen weight before the results are added.

citation.quotedText: the weighted sum of the values in according to the weights could be expressed as the dot product

analogies.cooking: Imagine multiplying each flavor score by how important that flavor is, then adding the results for one weighted recipe rating.
analogies.sports: Like multiplying each performance statistic by its chosen importance, then adding the products to create a weighted player score.
analogies.music: Imagine multiplying each track’s score by its chosen influence, then adding the products into a weighted playlist rating.
analogies.video-games: Like multiplying each skill score by its chosen importance, then adding the products for a weighted character rating.
analogies.cars: Imagine multiplying fuel economy, comfort, and safety scores by chosen importance levels, then adding them into a weighted car rating.
analogies.gardening: Like multiplying sunlight, soil, and water scores by chosen importance levels, then adding the products into a weighted plant score.

steps.text: If the weights are zero or positive and add up to 1, the result is a weighted average.

citation.quotedText: When the weights are nonnegative and sum to 1, i.e., , the dot product expresses a weighted average.

analogies.cooking: Imagine recipe ratings receiving nonnegative shares of importance totaling one; combining them produces a weighted average rating.
analogies.sports: Like assigning nonnegative importance shares totaling one to several game statistics, then combining them into a weighted average performance.
analogies.music: Imagine giving songs nonnegative influence shares totaling one, then combining their ratings into a weighted average playlist score.
analogies.video-games: Like giving character attributes nonnegative importance shares totaling one, then combining their scores into a weighted average rating.
analogies.cars: Imagine assigning nonnegative importance shares totaling one to car features, then combining their scores into a weighted average.
analogies.gardening: Like giving several plant-growth measures nonnegative importance shares totaling one, then combining their values into a weighted average.

concepts.title: Matrix–Vector Products
concepts.summary: A matrix–vector product produces a vector whose elements are dot products between matrix rows and the input vector.

provenance.quotedText: The matrix--vector product is simply a column vector of length m, whose element is the dot product : We can think of multiplication with a matrix as a transformation that projects vectors from to .

lesson.plainTitle: How a Matrix Changes a Vector

steps.text: Multiplying a matrix by a vector produces a new column vector. Each number in the result is a dot product—the sum found by multiplying matching values in one matrix row and the input vector, then adding them.

citation.quotedText: The matrix--vector product is simply a column vector of length m, whose element is the dot product

analogies.cooking: Imagine each recipe row pairing its ingredient amounts with your available ingredients, multiplying each match, then adding the results to create one new score.
analogies.sports: Like each player's statistics being matched with coaching weights, multiplied pair by pair, and added to produce one number in a new results column.
analogies.music: Imagine each mixer row adjusting matching instrument levels, then adding those contributions to produce one output level in a new sound vector.
analogies.video-games: Think of each damage formula matching character stats with multipliers, multiplying each pair, and adding them to create one value in a new results list.
analogies.cars: Imagine each performance formula pairing car measurements with weights, multiplying matching values, and adding them to produce one number in a new output column.
analogies.gardening: Like each garden plan matching quantities such as sunlight, water, and soil with weights, then adding the paired products to create one result.

steps.text: For the multiplication to work, each matrix row must have the same number of values as the input vector.

citation.quotedText: Note that the column dimension of A (its length along axis 1) must be the same as the dimension of x (its length).

analogies.cooking: Imagine a recipe checklist with exactly one slot for every ingredient you provide; missing or extra ingredients prevent all positions from pairing correctly.
analogies.sports: Like a lineup drill requiring one player for every assigned position; the counts must match before each position can be paired.
analogies.music: Imagine a mixer preset needing one control for every instrument channel; the numbers must match so each control has a channel.
analogies.video-games: Like equipping one item into every available gear slot; the item list and slot list must be equal in length.
analogies.cars: Imagine matching one tire-pressure reading to each wheel position; the lists need equal lengths for every reading to have a place.
analogies.gardening: Like planting one seed in each prepared hole; the number of seeds and holes must match for every pairing to work.

steps.text: You can view this multiplication as a transformation that changes or maps a vector. For example, certain square matrices can rotate vectors.

citation.quotedText: For example, we can represent rotations as multiplications by certain square matrices.

analogies.cooking: Imagine reshaping dough with a mold: the same dough becomes a new form, like a matrix mapping a vector to a changed direction or shape.
analogies.sports: Like a planned pass redirecting the ball across the field; the operation changes its direction, resembling a transformation that can rotate a vector.
analogies.music: Imagine a musical phrase transposed or shifted by a rule; its notes become a mapped version, like transforming a vector into a new one.
analogies.video-games: Like applying a rotation command to a character's direction arrow, mapping the original orientation to a new one.
analogies.cars: Imagine turning the steering wheel so the car's direction changes; this resembles a matrix transforming, and sometimes rotating, a vector.
analogies.gardening: Like guiding a vine around a trellis so its growth direction changes, illustrating how a transformation can map one direction to another.

steps.text: Matrix–vector products are also used in neural networks to calculate each layer’s output from the previous layer’s output.

citation.quotedText: Matrix--vector products also describe the key calculation involved in computing the outputs of each layer in a neural network given the outputs from the previous layer.

analogies.cooking: Imagine each kitchen station combining weighted portions from earlier stations to prepare its next output, like one neural-network layer using the previous layer’s results.
analogies.sports: Like each coaching stage combining weighted performance signals from the previous drill to produce scores that guide the next stage.
analogies.music: Imagine each effects unit mixing weighted signals from the previous unit to create its own outputs before passing them onward.
analogies.video-games: Like each processing stage combining weighted values from the previous stage to calculate new signals for the next stage.
analogies.cars: Imagine each control module combining weighted sensor outputs from an earlier module to calculate the values it passes forward.
analogies.gardening: Like each planning stage combining weighted observations from the previous stage to produce new recommendations for the next stage.

concepts.title: Positional Encoding
concepts.summary: Positional information is supplied as an additional token-associated input because self-attention alone does not preserve sequence order.

provenance.quotedText: The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

lesson.plainTitle: How a Model Keeps Track of Word Order

steps.text: A model is given extra information with each token, or piece of text, so it can keep track of where that token appears in the sequence.

citation.quotedText: The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

analogies.cooking: Imagine labeling each ingredient with its recipe-step number so the cook knows when it belongs.
analogies.sports: Like giving each player a numbered position marker so the team knows where everyone belongs in the formation.
analogies.music: Imagine attaching a measure and beat number to each note so its place in the song stays clear.
analogies.video-games: Think of each quest item carrying a slot number that shows where it appears in the mission sequence.
analogies.cars: Like numbering every part along an assembly line so its proper stage remains clear.
analogies.gardening: Imagine marking each seed with its row and spot so its location in the garden is known.

steps.text: This extra information is called positional encoding. It can be learned during training or set in advance using a fixed method.

citation.quotedText: These inputs are called positional encodings, and they can either be learned or fixed a priori.

analogies.cooking: Think of recipe-step labels that are either learned through repeated cooking or assigned beforehand by a standard numbering system.
analogies.sports: Like position markers that a team either learns during practice or receives from a preset play diagram.
analogies.music: Imagine timing cues that musicians either learn through rehearsal or follow from a fixed written score.
analogies.video-games: Like map coordinates that a game either learns to use during development or calculates with a preset system.
analogies.cars: Think of assembly-stage codes that are either learned from production data or assigned using a fixed scheme.
analogies.gardening: Imagine plot labels that are either learned from past planting patterns or set beforehand with a regular grid.

steps.text: One fixed method uses repeating sine and cosine patterns to represent positions.

citation.quotedText: We now describe a simple scheme for fixed positional encodings based on sine and cosine functions .

analogies.cooking: Imagine marking recipe steps with two repeating kitchen-timer rhythms that together indicate each step’s place.
analogies.sports: Like using two repeating scoreboard-light patterns to give each moment in a play a distinctive position signal.
analogies.music: Imagine two smooth, repeating musical waves working together to mark places throughout a composition.
analogies.video-games: Think of two looping animation patterns combining to signal each location along a game level.
analogies.cars: Like two repeating dashboard signals combining to mark different points along a route.
analogies.gardening: Imagine two repeating watering patterns overlapping to identify positions across rows of plants.

steps.text: Different parts of the encoding repeat at different rates, helping distinguish positions much like binary digits change at different rates.

citation.quotedText: In binary representations, a higher bit has a lower frequency than a lower bit. Similarly, as demonstrated in the heat map below, the positional encoding decreases frequencies along the encoding dimension by using trigonometric functions.

analogies.cooking: Imagine several kitchen timers ticking at different speeds; their combined states help identify the current recipe step.
analogies.sports: Like several stadium clocks cycling at different rates, creating a combined pattern that separates one play moment from another.
analogies.music: Imagine rhythms of different speeds playing together, with their changing combination marking distinct places in the song.
analogies.video-games: Like several rotating beacons moving at different speeds, whose combined pattern identifies where you are in a level.
analogies.cars: Imagine dashboard counters turning at different rates, like odometer digits, so their combination marks a particular point.
analogies.gardening: Like watering wheels rotating at different speeds, creating combined patterns that distinguish one garden position from another.

concepts.title: Queries, Keys, and Values
concepts.summary: Attention derives weights from query–key compatibility and uses those weights to form a linear combination of values.

provenance.quotedText: The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values via attention pooling, where weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query and keys .

lesson.plainTitle: How Attention Chooses and Combines Information

steps.text: Attention compares a query—the information being searched for—with keys that label available information. It turns each match into a weight, then uses those weights to combine the corresponding values.

citation.quotedText: The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values via attention pooling, where weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query and keys .

analogies.cooking: Imagine choosing ingredients by matching a recipe’s needs to pantry labels, then blending each ingredient according to how well it matches.
analogies.sports: Think of a coach matching a game plan to player roles, then combining each player’s contribution according to the strength of the match.
analogies.music: Imagine matching a desired sound to labeled instrument tracks, then mixing those tracks with weights based on how well each fits.
analogies.video-games: Like matching a quest objective to inventory labels, then combining the useful items’ effects according to how relevant each item is.
analogies.cars: Imagine matching a driving need to dashboard controls, then combining their effects according to how closely each control fits the situation.
analogies.gardening: Think of matching a garden’s needs to labeled supplies, then combining water, compost, and nutrients according to how well each matches.

steps.text: When the weights are zero or greater and add up to 1, larger weights can be viewed as stronger focus on the most relevant pieces. This is a helpful interpretation, not a guaranteed explanation of the model.

citation.quotedText: In this case we might interpret large weights as a way for the model to select components of relevance. While this is a good intuition, it is important to remember that it is just that, an intuition.

analogies.cooking: Imagine a sauce recipe where nonnegative portions total one whole batch; larger portions suggest stronger flavor emphasis, though they do not fully explain the chef’s reasoning.
analogies.sports: Like dividing a team’s focus across opponents so the shares total 100%; a larger share suggests more attention, not a guaranteed account of the strategy.
analogies.music: Imagine mixing tracks with nonnegative volume shares totaling one; louder shares suggest stronger focus, though they do not completely explain the musical decision.
analogies.video-games: Think of distributing targeting priority across enemies so it totals 100%; a larger share suggests stronger focus, not proof of why the game chose it.
analogies.cars: Imagine distributing traction among wheels with shares totaling one; a larger share suggests greater emphasis, though it does not fully explain the control system.
analogies.gardening: Like dividing watering across plants so the shares total one; larger shares suggest stronger focus on certain plants, not a guaranteed explanation of the gardener’s choice.

steps.text: The same attention process can search through collections of different sizes without changing how it combines the information.

citation.quotedText: attention can operate on arbitrarily large databases without the need to change the way the attention pooling operation is performed.

analogies.cooking: Imagine using the same tasting-and-blending method whether choosing from three ingredients or thirty; only the pantry size changes, not the combining process.
analogies.sports: Like a coach using the same selection method with a small squad or a large roster; the number of players changes, but the combination approach stays.
analogies.music: Imagine using the same mixing method for a trio or an orchestra; there are more tracks to consider, but each is still weighted and combined.
analogies.video-games: Like searching a small inventory or a huge vault with the same selection system; the collection grows, while the way chosen effects combine remains unchanged.
analogies.cars: Imagine the same navigation process comparing a few routes or many routes; the number of options changes, but weighted information is combined the same way.
analogies.gardening: Think of choosing plants from one bed or an entire nursery using the same method; the collection size changes, but the selections are combined similarly.

concepts.title: Self-Attention
concepts.summary: Self-attention lets every token attend to every other token in the same sequence.

provenance.quotedText: Because every token is attending to each other token (unlike the case where decoder steps attend to encoder steps), such architectures are typically described as self-attention models , and elsewhere described as intra-attention model .

lesson.plainTitle: How each word can use the whole sequence

steps.text: In self-attention, every token—a word or word piece—can look at every other token in the same sequence to decide what information matters.

citation.quotedText: Because every token is attending to each other token

analogies.cooking: Imagine each ingredient checking every other ingredient before deciding how strongly its flavor should contribute to the dish.
analogies.sports: Like every player watching all teammates and opponents to decide which movements matter for the next play.
analogies.music: Imagine each note listening across the whole phrase to decide which other notes shape its role.
analogies.video-games: Think of each character scanning every character on the map to decide whose actions are relevant.
analogies.cars: Imagine each car sensor considering readings from all other sensors before deciding what information matters.
analogies.gardening: Like each plant being considered alongside every other plant when deciding its role in the garden layout.

steps.text: Self-attention produces an output sequence with the same number of positions as the input sequence.

citation.quotedText: its self-attention outputs a sequence of the same length

analogies.cooking: Imagine a tray entering with twelve portions and leaving with twelve improved portions, each enriched by the whole recipe.
analogies.sports: Like a lineup of eleven players remaining eleven players after each receives updated tactical instructions.
analogies.music: Imagine eight notes entering an arrangement and eight notes emerging, each with a newly informed musical role.
analogies.video-games: Like a squad keeping the same number of characters after every member receives updated information about the match.
analogies.cars: Imagine six dashboard signals going into a system and six updated signals coming out in matching positions.
analogies.gardening: Like ten garden plots remaining ten plots after each plan is updated using information from the entire garden.

steps.text: Because information can travel directly between any two positions, self-attention can make relationships between far-apart words easier to learn.

citation.quotedText: a shorter path between any combination of sequence positions makes it easier to learn long-range dependencies within the sequence

analogies.cooking: Imagine a spice added early directly influencing a finishing sauce, without its effect passing through every intermediate step.
analogies.sports: Like a goalkeeper sending a direct pass to a distant striker, making their connection easier to establish.
analogies.music: Imagine the opening note directly guiding the final chord, making their long-range relationship easier to recognize.
analogies.video-games: Like two players on opposite sides of a map communicating directly instead of relaying messages through every teammate.
analogies.cars: Imagine the rear sensor directly informing the front display, avoiding a long chain of intermediate components.
analogies.gardening: Like comparing plants at opposite ends of a garden directly, making their shared watering needs easier to notice.

concepts.title: Softmax
concepts.summary: Softmax transforms neural-network outputs into a valid probability distribution over discrete classes.

provenance.quotedText: As a side effect, we encountered the softmax, a convenient activation function that transforms outputs of an ordinary neural network layer into valid discrete probability distributions.

steps.text: Softmax is a function that turns a neural network’s output scores into probabilities for a set of separate classes, such as cat, dog, or bird.

citation.quotedText: the softmax, a convenient activation function that transforms outputs of an ordinary neural network layer into valid discrete probability distributions.

analogies.cooking: Imagine turning judges’ scores for separate dishes into percentage chances that each dish will win.
analogies.sports: Like converting team ratings into win probabilities that together cover all possible teams.
analogies.music: Imagine transforming scores for different songs into percentage chances that each song is the listener’s choice.
analogies.video-games: Think of converting character scores into probabilities for which character class was selected.
analogies.cars: Like turning ratings for separate car models into percentage chances that each model matches the driver’s choice.
analogies.gardening: Imagine converting scores for different flower types into probabilities for which flower appears in a photo.

steps.text: When softmax is paired with cross-entropy loss, learning uses the difference between the expected result and the network’s prediction.

citation.quotedText: We saw that the derivative of the cross-entropy loss when combined with softmax behaves very similarly to the derivative of squared error; namely by taking the difference between the expected behavior and its prediction.

analogies.cooking: Imagine comparing the expected winning dish with predicted chances, then adjusting the recipe based on the mismatch.
analogies.sports: Like comparing the actual winner with predicted team probabilities, then using the error to improve future predictions.
analogies.music: Imagine comparing the expected note with predicted note probabilities, then adjusting practice according to the mismatch.
analogies.video-games: Like comparing the correct target with predicted choices, then using the mistake signal to improve the next attempt.
analogies.cars: Imagine comparing the expected car model with predicted probabilities, then tuning the system based on the difference.
analogies.gardening: Like comparing the expected plant type with predicted probabilities, then using the mismatch to refine future identification.

concepts.title: Softmax Preserves Ordering
concepts.summary: Softmax preserves the ordering of its inputs, so the largest input identifies the highest-probability class.

provenance.quotedText: Moreover, because the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments, we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability.

lesson.plainTitle: The Biggest Input Stays on Top

steps.text: Softmax turns input scores into probabilities without changing their order: a higher score still produces a higher probability.

citation.quotedText: the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments

analogies.cooking: Imagine ranking dishes by judges’ scores, then converting those scores into menu popularity shares; the higher-scored dish still gets the larger share.
analogies.sports: Like turning race points into percentages of the total: athletes keep the same ranking, so higher points mean a higher percentage.
analogies.music: Imagine converting song ratings into playlist probabilities; a song with a higher rating still has a higher chance of being selected.
analogies.video-games: Like changing player power scores into selection chances; the player with more power still receives the greater chance.
analogies.cars: Imagine converting car performance scores into preference probabilities; a higher-scoring car remains more likely to be chosen.
analogies.gardening: Like turning flower-show scores into winning probabilities; the flower with the higher score still receives the higher probability.

steps.text: To find the class with the highest probability, simply choose the class with the largest input score. You do not need to calculate all the probabilities first.

citation.quotedText: we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability

analogies.cooking: Imagine choosing the judges’ favorite dish by finding the highest score; you need not calculate every dish’s share of the total first.
analogies.sports: Like identifying the leader by checking who has the most points, without first converting every player’s score into a percentage.
analogies.music: Imagine picking the top-rated song directly from its rating; there is no need to compute every song’s playlist probability.
analogies.video-games: Like selecting the strongest character by comparing power scores; choose the largest score without calculating every selection chance.
analogies.cars: Imagine choosing the leading car by its highest performance score, without converting all car scores into preference probabilities.
analogies.gardening: Like finding the likely flower-show winner by choosing the highest judge score, without calculating every flower’s winning probability.

concepts.title: Vector Norms
concepts.summary: A vector norm measures the magnitude or size of a vector.

provenance.quotedText: Informally, the norm of a vector tells us how big it is.

steps.text: A vector norm is a number that tells you the vector’s size or length.

citation.quotedText: Informally, the norm of a vector tells us how big it is.

analogies.cooking: Imagine a single score describing how large a batch of ingredients is overall.
analogies.sports: Think of one number summarizing the length of an athlete’s movement from start to finish.
analogies.music: Imagine one number expressing the overall size of a musical control vector.
analogies.video-games: Like a stat that summarizes the overall size of a character’s movement vector.
analogies.cars: Think of one gauge reading that summarizes the size of a car’s motion vector.
analogies.gardening: Imagine one number summarizing the overall size of several measured garden changes.

steps.text: It measures the size of the vector’s component values, not how many components the vector has.

citation.quotedText: Here, we are employing a notion of size that concerns the magnitude of a vector's components (not its dimensionality).

analogies.cooking: Like judging a recipe by the ingredient amounts, not simply by counting how many ingredients are listed.
analogies.sports: Imagine measuring the strength of recorded performance values, rather than counting how many statistics appear.
analogies.music: Think of judging signal levels by their values, not by the number of tracks.
analogies.video-games: Like measuring the amounts in a movement command, not merely counting its directional components.
analogies.cars: Imagine assessing driving measurements by their values, not by how many readings the dashboard provides.
analogies.gardening: Think of measuring the amounts of growth data, rather than counting the number of measurements.

steps.text: One common norm finds the vector’s length by squaring each component, adding the squares, and taking the square root.

citation.quotedText: The Euclidean norm that we all learned in elementary school geometry when calculating the hypotenuse of a right triangle is the square root of the sum of squares of a vector's elements.

analogies.cooking: Imagine squaring each ingredient amount, adding those results, then taking the square root to create one overall measure.
analogies.sports: Like squaring each directional movement, adding the squares, and taking the square root to measure the overall displacement.
analogies.music: Imagine squaring several signal values, totaling them, and taking the square root for one combined measure.
analogies.video-games: Think of squaring horizontal and vertical movement, adding them, then taking the square root to find movement length.
analogies.cars: Like squaring motion values in different directions, adding them, and taking the square root for overall length.
analogies.gardening: Imagine squaring several growth changes, adding the squares, and taking the square root for one combined measure.

steps.text: Another norm adds the absolute values of all components. Different norms can therefore represent different ideas of size.

citation.quotedText: By definition, the norm sums the absolute values of a vector's elements:

analogies.cooking: Imagine adding every ingredient amount without letting negative adjustments cancel positive ones; this gives a different idea of total size.
analogies.sports: Like adding the magnitudes of movements in every direction, producing a different size measure from straight-line distance.
analogies.music: Think of adding each control change by magnitude, ignoring whether it rises or falls, to represent size differently.
analogies.video-games: Imagine adding all directional movement amounts without signs; this measures total grid-like travel rather than straight-line distance.
analogies.cars: Like adding the magnitudes of separate directional changes, giving a different view of movement size.
analogies.gardening: Think of adding every increase or decrease by its magnitude, so all changes contribute to a different measure of total size.

concepts.title: Vectors
concepts.summary: A vector is a fixed-length array whose elements are scalars.

provenance.quotedText: For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars.

steps.text: A vector is a list with a set number of scalar values. A scalar is a single number.

citation.quotedText: For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars.

analogies.cooking: Imagine a recipe card with exactly three numbered amounts; each amount is one number, and together they form a fixed-length list.
analogies.sports: Think of a player stat line containing exactly four numbers, such as points, assists, rebounds, and steals.
analogies.music: Like a short rhythm pattern with exactly four numbered beat lengths, a vector holds a set number of individual numbers.
analogies.video-games: Imagine a character status panel with exactly three numbers for health, armor, and speed.
analogies.cars: Think of a dashboard record with exactly four numbers for speed, fuel, temperature, and mileage.
analogies.gardening: Like a planting note with exactly three numbers for water, sunlight, and spacing.

steps.text: Each value in a vector is called an element; it may also be called an entry or component.

citation.quotedText: As with their code counterparts, we call these scalars the elements of the vector (synonyms include entries and components).

analogies.cooking: Imagine each numbered amount on a recipe card as one element, also called an entry or component.
analogies.sports: Think of each number in a player stat line as an element, entry, or component of the complete list.
analogies.music: Like each numbered beat length in a rhythm pattern, every value is an element, entry, or component.
analogies.video-games: Imagine health as one element, armor as another, and speed as another in a character’s fixed list.
analogies.cars: Think of speed, fuel, and temperature readings as separate elements, entries, or components in one list.
analogies.gardening: Like water, sunlight, and spacing measurements, each number is an element, entry, or component of the garden list.

steps.text: A vector can describe one real-world example, such as a loan applicant. Its elements might record income, employment length, and past defaults.

citation.quotedText: For example, if we were training a model to predict the risk of a loan defaulting, we might associate each applicant with a vector whose components correspond to quantities like their income, length of employment, or number of previous defaults.

analogies.cooking: Imagine one dish represented by a vector whose elements record cooking time, oven temperature, and serving count.
analogies.sports: Think of one athlete represented by a vector recording points, assists, and rebounds.
analogies.music: Imagine one song represented by a vector whose elements record tempo, duration, and track count.
analogies.video-games: Think of one game character represented by a vector recording health, armor, and experience.
analogies.cars: Imagine one car represented by a vector whose elements record mileage, fuel level, and engine temperature.
analogies.gardening: Think of one plant represented by a vector recording height, daily water, and hours of sunlight.

sources.title: Dive into Deep Learning — 2.3 Linear Algebra
sources.author: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola
sources.text: # Linear Algebra By now, we can load datasets into tensors and manipulate these tensors with basic mathematical operations. To start building sophisticated models, we will also need a few tools from linear algebra. This section offers a gentle introduction to the most essential concepts, starting from scalar arithmetic and ramping up to matrix multiplication. ## Scalars Most everyday mathematics consists of manipulating numbers one at a time. Formally, we call these values scalars. For example, the temperature in Palo Alto is a balmy 72 degrees Fahrenheit. If you wanted to convert the temperature to Celsius you would evaluate the expression , setting f to 72. In this equation, the values 5, 9, and 32 are constant scalars. The variables c and f in general represent unknown scalars. We denote scalars by ordinary lower-cased letters (e.g., x, y, and z) and the space of all (continuous) real-valued scalars by . For expedience, we will skip past rigorous definitions of spaces: just remember that the expression is a formal way to say that x is a real-valued scalar. The symbol (pronounced "in") denotes membership in a set. For example, indicates that x and y are variables that can only take values 0 or 1. (Scalars are implemented as tensors that contain only one element.) Below, we assign two scalars and perform the familiar addition, multiplication, division, and exponentiation operations. ## Vectors For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars. As with their code counterparts, we call these scalars the elements of the vector (synonyms include entries and components). When vectors represent examples from real-world datasets, their values hold some real-world significance. For example, if we were training a model to predict the risk of a loan defaulting, we might associate each applicant with a vector whose components correspond to quantities like their income, length of employment, or number of previous defaults. If we were studying the risk of heart attack, each vector might represent a patient and its components might correspond to their most recent vital signs, cholesterol levels, minutes of exercise per day, etc. We denote vectors by bold lowercase letters, (e.g., , , and ). Vectors are implemented as -order tensors. In general, such tensors can have arbitrary lengths, subject to memory limitations. Caution: in Python, as in most programming languages, vector indices start at 0, also known as zero-based indexing, whereas in linear algebra subscripts begin at 1 (one-based indexing). We can refer to an element of a vector by using a subscript. For example, denotes the second element of . Since is a scalar, we do not bold it. By default, we visualize vectors by stacking their elements vertically. Here are elements of the vector. Later on, we will distinguish between such column vectors and row vectors whose elements are stacked horizontally. Recall that we access a tensor's elements via indexing. To indicate that a vector contains n elements, we write . Formally, we call n the dimensionality of the vector. In code, this corresponds to the tensor's length, accessible via Python's built-in len function. We can also access the length via the shape attribute. The shape is a tuple that indicates a tensor's length along each axis. (Tensors with just one axis have shapes with just one element.) Oftentimes, the word "dimension" gets overloaded to mean both the number of axes and the length along a particular axis. To avoid this confusion, we use order to refer to the number of axes and dimensionality exclusively to refer to the number of components. ## Matrices Just as scalars are -order tensors and vectors are -order tensors, matrices are -order tensors. We denote matrices by bold capital letters (e.g., , , and ), and represent them in code by tensors with two axes. The expression indicates that a matrix contains real-valued scalars, arranged as m rows and n columns. When , we say that a matrix is square. Visually, we can illustrate any matrix as a table. To refer to an individual element, we subscript both the row and column indices, e.g., is the value that belongs to 's row and column: In code, we represent a matrix by a -order tensor with shape (m, n). We can convert any appropriately sized tensor into an matrix by passing the desired shape to reshape: Sometimes we want to flip the axes. When we exchange a matrix's rows and columns, the result is called its transpose. Formally, we signify a matrix 's transpose by and if , then for all i and j. Thus, the transpose of an matrix is an matrix: In code, we can access any (matrix's transpose) as follows: Symmetric matrices are the subset of square matrices that are equal to their own transposes: . The following matrix is symmetric: Matrices are useful for representing datasets. Typically, rows correspond to individual records and columns correspond to distinct attributes. ## Tensors While you can go far in your machine learning journey with only scalars, vectors, and matrices, eventually you may need to work with higher-order tensors. Tensors (give us a generic way of describing extensions to -order arrays.) We call software objects of the tensor class "tensors" precisely because they too can have arbitrary numbers of axes. While it may be confusing to use the word tensor for both the mathematical object and its realization in code, our meaning should usually be clear from context. We denote general tensors by capital letters with a special font face (e.g., , , and ) and their indexing mechanism (e.g., and ) follows naturally from that of matrices. Tensors will become more important when we start working with images. Each image arrives as a -order tensor with axes corresponding to the height, width, and channel. At each spatial location, the intensities of each color (red, green, and blue) are stacked along the channel. Furthermore, a collection of images is represented in code by a -order tensor, where distinct images are indexed along the first axis. Higher-order tensors are constructed, as were vectors and matrices, by growing the number of shape components. ## Basic Properties of Tensor Arithmetic Scalars, vectors, matrices, and higher-order tensors all have some handy properties. For example, elementwise operations produce outputs that have the same shape as their operands. The elementwise product of two matrices is called their Hadamard product (denoted ). We can spell out the entries of the Hadamard product of two matrices : Adding or multiplying a scalar and a tensor produces a result with the same shape as the original tensor. Here, each element of the tensor is added to (or multiplied by) the scalar. ## Reduction Often, we wish to calculate the sum of a tensor's elements. To express the sum of the elements in a vector of length n, we write . There is a simple function for it: To express sums over the elements of tensors of arbitrary shape, we simply sum over all its axes. For example, the sum of the elements of an matrix could be written . By default, invoking the sum function reduces a tensor along all of its axes, eventually producing a scalar. Our libraries also allow us to specify the axes along which the tensor should be reduced. To sum over all elements along the rows (axis 0), we specify axis=0 in sum. Since the input matrix reduces along axis 0 to generate the output vector, this axis is missing from the shape of the output. Specifying axis=1 will reduce the column dimension (axis 1) by summing up elements of all the columns. Reducing a matrix along both rows and columns via summation is equivalent to summing up all the elements of the matrix. A related quantity is the mean, also called the average. We calculate the mean by dividing the sum by the total number of elements. Because computing the mean is so common, it gets a dedicated library function that works analogously to sum. Likewise, the function for calculating the mean can also reduce a tensor along specific axes. ## Non-Reduction Sum Sometimes it can be useful to keep the number of axes unchanged when invoking the function for calculating the sum or mean. This matters when we want to use the broadcast mechanism. For instance, since sum_A keeps its two axes after summing each row, we can (divide A by sum_A with broadcasting) to create a matrix where each row sums up to 1. If we want to calculate the cumulative sum of elements of A along some axis, say axis=0 (row by row), we can call the cumsum function. By design, this function does not reduce the input tensor along any axis. ## Dot Products So far, we have only performed elementwise operations, sums, and averages. And if this was all we could do, linear algebra would not deserve its own section. Fortunately, this is where things get more interesting. One of the most fundamental operations is the dot product. Given two vectors , their dot product (also known as inner product, ) is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position: . The dot product of two vectors is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position Equivalently, (we can calculate the dot product of two vectors by performing an elementwise multiplication followed by a sum:) Dot products are useful in a wide range of contexts. For example, given some set of values, denoted by a vector , and a set of weights, denoted by , the weighted sum of the values in according to the weights could be expressed as the dot product . When the weights are nonnegative and sum to 1, i.e., , the dot product expresses a weighted average. After normalizing two vectors to have unit length, the dot products express the cosine of the angle between them. Later in this section, we will formally introduce this notion of length. ## Matrix--Vector Products Now that we know how to calculate dot products, we can begin to understand the product between an matrix and an n-dimensional vector . To start off, we visualize our matrix in terms of its row vectors where each is a row vector representing the row of the matrix . The matrix--vector product is simply a column vector of length m, whose element is the dot product : We can think of multiplication with a matrix as a transformation that projects vectors from to . These transformations are remarkably useful. For example, we can represent rotations as multiplications by certain square matrices. Matrix--vector products also describe the key calculation involved in computing the outputs of each layer in a neural network given the outputs from the previous layer. :begin_tab:mxnet To express a matrix--vector product in code, we use the same dot function. The operation is inferred based on the type of the arguments. Note that the column dimension of A (its length along axis 1) must be the same as the dimension of x (its length). :end_tab: :begin_tab:pytorch To express a matrix--vector product in code, we use the mv function. Note that the column dimension of A (its length along axis 1) must be the same as the dimension of x (its length). Python has a convenience operator @ that can execute both matrix--vector and matrix--matrix products (depending on its arguments). Thus we can write A@x. :end_tab: :begin_tab:tensorflow To express a matrix--vector product in code, we use the matvec function. Note that the column dimension of A (its length along axis 1) must be the same as the dimension of x (its length). :end_tab: ## Matrix--Matrix Multiplication Once you have gotten the hang of dot products and matrix--vector products, then matrix--matrix multiplication should be straightforward. Say that we have two matrices and : Let denote the row vector representing the row of the matrix and let denote the column vector from the column of the matrix : To form the matrix product , we simply compute each element as the dot product between the row of and the column of , i.e., : We can think of the matrix--matrix multiplication as performing m matrix--vector products or dot products and stitching the results together to form an matrix. In the following snippet, we perform matrix multiplication on A and B. Here, A is a matrix with two rows and three columns, and B is a matrix with three rows and four columns. After multiplication, we obtain a matrix with two rows and four columns. The term matrix--matrix multiplication is often simplified to matrix multiplication, and should not be confused with the Hadamard product. ## Norms Some of the most useful operators in linear algebra are norms. Informally, the norm of a vector tells us how big it is. For instance, the norm measures the (Euclidean) length of a vector. Here, we are employing a notion of size that concerns the magnitude of a vector's components (not its dimensionality). A norm is a function that maps a vector to a scalar and satisfies the following three properties: 1. Given any vector , if we scale (all elements of) the vector by a scalar , its norm scales accordingly: 2. For any vectors and : norms satisfy the triangle inequality: 3. The norm of a vector is nonnegative and it only vanishes if the vector is zero: Many functions are valid norms and different norms encode different notions of size. The Euclidean norm that we all learned in elementary school geometry when calculating the hypotenuse of a right triangle is the square root of the sum of squares of a vector's elements. Formally, this is called the norm and expressed as ( ) The method norm calculates the norm. The norm is also common and the associated measure is called the Manhattan distance. By definition, the norm sums the absolute values of a vector's elements: ( ) Compared to the norm, it is less sensitive to outliers. To compute the norm, we compose the absolute value with the sum operation. Both the and norms are special cases of the more general norms: In the case of matrices, matters are more complicated. After all, matrices can be viewed both as collections of individual entries and as objects that operate on vectors and transform them into other vectors. For instance, we can ask by how much longer the matrix--vector product could be relative to . This line of thought leads to what is called the spectral norm. For now, we introduce the Frobenius norm, which is much easier to compute and defined as the square root of the sum of the squares of a matrix's elements: The Frobenius norm behaves as if it were an norm of a matrix-shaped vector. Invoking the following function will calculate the Frobenius norm of a matrix. While we do not want to get too far ahead of ourselves, we already can plant some intuition about why these concepts are useful. In deep learning, we are often trying to solve optimization problems: maximize the probability assigned to observed data; maximize the revenue associated with a recommender model; minimize the distance between predictions and the ground truth observations; minimize the distance between representations of photos of the same person while maximizing the distance between representations of photos of different people. These distances, which constitute the objectives of deep learning algorithms, are often expressed as norms. ## Discussion In this section, we have reviewed all the linear algebra that you will need to understand a significant chunk of modern deep learning. There is a lot more to linear algebra, though, and much of it is useful for machine learning. For example, matrices can be decomposed into factors, and these decompositions can reveal low-dimensional structure in real-world datasets. There are entire subfields of machine learning that focus on using matrix decompositions and their generalizations to high-order tensors to discover structure in datasets and solve prediction problems. But this book focuses on deep learning. And we believe you will be more inclined to learn more mathematics once you have gotten your hands dirty applying machine learning to real datasets. So while we reserve the right to introduce more mathematics later on, we wrap up this section here. If you are eager to learn more linear algebra, there are many excellent books and online resources. For a more advanced crash course, consider checking out , , and . To recap: Scalars, vectors, matrices, and tensors are the basic mathematical objects used in linear algebra and have zero, one, two, and an arbitrary number of axes, respectively. Tensors can be sliced or reduced along specified axes via indexing, or operations such as sum and mean, respectively. Elementwise products are called Hadamard products. By contrast, dot products, matrix--vector products, and matrix--matrix products are not elementwise operations and in general return objects having shapes that are different from the the operands. Compared to Hadamard products, matrix--matrix products take considerably longer to compute (cubic rather than quadratic time). Norms capture various notions of the magnitude of a vector (or matrix), and are commonly applied to the difference of two vectors to measure their distance apart. Common vector norms include the and norms, and common matrix norms include the spectral and Frobenius norms. ## Exercises 1. Prove that the transpose of the transpose of a matrix is the matrix itself: . 1. Given two matrices and , show that sum and transposition commute: . 1. Given any square matrix , is always symmetric? Can you prove the result by using only the results of the previous two exercises? 1. We defined the tensor X of shape (2, 3, 4) in this section. What is the output of len(X)? Write your answer without implementing any code, then check your answer using code. 1. For a tensor X of arbitrary shape, does len(X) always correspond to the length of a certain axis of X? What is that axis? 1. Run A / A.sum(axis=1) and see what happens. Can you analyze the results? 1. When traveling between two points in downtown Manhattan, what is the distance that you need to cover in terms of the coordinates, i.e., in terms of avenues and streets? Can you travel diagonally? 1. Consider a tensor of shape (2, 3, 4). What are the shapes of the summation outputs along axes 0, 1, and 2? 1. Feed a tensor with three or more axes to the linalg.norm function and observe its output. What does this function compute for tensors of arbitrary shape? 1. Consider three large matrices, say , and , initialized with Gaussian random variables. You want to compute the product . Is there any difference in memory footprint and speed, depending on whether you compute or . Why? 1. Consider three large matrices, say , and . Is there any difference in speed depending on whether you compute or ? Why? What changes if you initialize without cloning memory? Why? 1. Consider three matrices, say . Construct a tensor with three axes by stacking . What is the dimensionality? Slice out the second coordinate of the third axis to recover . Check that your answer is correct. :begin_tab:mxnet Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:pytorch Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:tensorflow Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:jax Discussions :end_tab:

sources.title: Dive into Deep Learning — 11.1 Queries, Keys, and Values
sources.author: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola
sources.text: # Queries, Keys, and Values So far all the networks we have reviewed crucially relied on the input being of a well-defined size. For instance, the images in ImageNet are of size pixels and CNNs are specifically tuned to this size. Even in natural language processing the input size for RNNs is well defined and fixed. Variable size is addressed by sequentially processing one token at a time, or by specially designed convolution kernels . This approach can lead to significant problems when the input is truly of varying size with varying information content, such as in in the transformation of text . In particular, for long sequences it becomes quite difficult to keep track of everything that has already been generated or even viewed by the network. Even explicit tracking heuristics such as proposed by only offer limited benefit. Compare this to databases. In their simplest form they are collections of keys (k) and values (v). For instance, our database might consist of tuples \{("Zhang", "Aston"), ("Lipton", "Zachary"), ("Li", "Mu"), ("Smola", "Alex"), ("Hu", "Rachel"), ("Werness", "Brent")\} with the last name being the key and the first name being the value. We can operate on , for instance with the exact query (q) for "Li" which would return the value "Mu". If ("Li", "Mu") was not a record in , there would be no valid answer. If we also allowed for approximate matches, we would retrieve ("Lipton", "Zachary") instead. This quite simple and trivial example nonetheless teaches us a number of useful things: We can design queries q that operate on (k,v) pairs in such a manner as to be valid regardless of the database size. The same query can receive different answers, according to the contents of the database. The "code" being executed for operating on a large state space (the database) can be quite simple (e.g., exact match, approximate match, top-k). There is no need to compress or simplify the database to make the operations effective. Clearly we would not have introduced a simple database here if it wasn't for the purpose of explaining deep learning. Indeed, this leads to one of the most exciting concepts introduced in deep learning in the past decade: the attention mechanism . We will cover the specifics of its application to machine translation later. For now, simply consider the following: denote by a database of m tuples of keys and values. Moreover, denote by a query. Then we can define the attention over as where ( ) are scalar attention weights. The operation itself is typically referred to as attention pooling. The name attention derives from the fact that the operation pays particular attention to the terms for which the weight is significant (i.e., large). As such, the attention over generates a linear combination of values contained in the database. In fact, this contains the above example as a special case where all but one weight is zero. We have a number of special cases: The weights are nonnegative. In this case the output of the attention mechanism is contained in the convex cone spanned by the values . The weights form a convex combination, i.e., and for all i. This is the most common setting in deep learning. Exactly one of the weights is 1, while all others are 0. This is akin to a traditional database query. All weights are equal, i.e., for all i. This amounts to averaging across the entire database, also called average pooling in deep learning. A common strategy for ensuring that the weights sum up to 1 is to normalize them via In particular, to ensure that the weights are also nonnegative, one can resort to exponentiation. This means that we can now pick any function and then apply the softmax operation used for multinomial models to it via This operation is readily available in all deep learning frameworks. It is differentiable and its gradient never vanishes, all of which are desirable properties in a model. Note though, the attention mechanism introduced above is not the only option. For instance, we can design a non-differentiable attention model that can be trained using reinforcement learning methods . As one would expect, training such a model is quite complex. Consequently the bulk of modern attention research follows the framework outlined in . We thus focus our exposition on this family of differentiable mechanisms. The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values via attention pooling, where weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query and keys . What is quite remarkable is that the actual "code" for executing on the set of keys and values, namely the query, can be quite concise, even though the space to operate on is significant. This is a desirable property for a network layer as it does not require too many parameters to learn. Just as convenient is the fact that attention can operate on arbitrarily large databases without the need to change the way the attention pooling operation is performed. ## Visualization One of the benefits of the attention mechanism is that it can be quite intuitive, particularly when the weights are nonnegative and sum to 1. In this case we might interpret large weights as a way for the model to select components of relevance. While this is a good intuition, it is important to remember that it is just that, an intuition. Regardless, we may want to visualize its effect on the given set of keys when applying a variety of different queries. This function will come in handy later. We thus define the show_heatmaps function. Note that it does not take a matrix (of attention weights) as its input but rather a tensor with four axes, allowing for an array of different queries and weights. Consequently the input matrices has the shape (number of rows for display, number of columns for display, number of queries, number of keys). This will come in handy later on when we want to visualize the workings that are to design Transformers. As a quick sanity check let's visualize the identity matrix, representing a case where the attention weight is 1 only when the query and the key are the same. ## Summary The attention mechanism allows us to aggregate data from many (key, value) pairs. So far our discussion was quite abstract, simply describing a way to pool data. We have not explained yet where those mysterious queries, keys, and values might arise from. Some intuition might help here: for instance, in a regression setting, the query might correspond to the location where the regression should be carried out. The keys are the locations where past data was observed and the values are the (regression) values themselves. This is the so-called Nadaraya--Watson estimator that we will be studying in the next section. By design, the attention mechanism provides a differentiable means of control by which a neural network can select elements from a set and to construct an associated weighted sum over representations. ## Exercises 1. Suppose that you wanted to reimplement approximate (key, query) matches as used in classical databases, which attention function would you pick? 1. Suppose that the attention function is given by and that for . Denote by the probability distribution over keys when using the softmax normalization in . Prove that . 1. Design a differentiable search engine using the attention mechanism. 1. Review the design of the Squeeze and Excitation Networks and interpret them through the lens of the attention mechanism. :begin_tab:mxnet Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:pytorch Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:tensorflow Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:jax Discussions :end_tab:

sources.title: Dive into Deep Learning — 11.6 Self-Attention and Positional Encoding
sources.author: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola
sources.text: # Self-Attention and Positional Encoding In deep learning, we often use CNNs or RNNs to encode sequences. Now with attention mechanisms in mind, imagine feeding a sequence of tokens into an attention mechanism such that at every step, each token has its own query, keys, and values. Here, when computing the value of a token's representation at the next layer, the token can attend (via its query vector) to any other's token (matching based on their key vectors). Using the full set of query-key compatibility scores, we can compute, for each token, a representation by building the appropriate weighted sum over the other tokens. Because every token is attending to each other token (unlike the case where decoder steps attend to encoder steps), such architectures are typically described as self-attention models , and elsewhere described as intra-attention model . In this section, we will discuss sequence encoding using self-attention, including using additional information for the sequence order. ## Self-Attention Given a sequence of input tokens where any ( ), its self-attention outputs a sequence of the same length , where according to the definition of attention pooling in . Using multi-head attention, the following code snippet computes the self-attention of a tensor with shape (batch size, number of time steps or sequence length in tokens, d). The output tensor has the same shape. ## Comparing CNNs, RNNs, and Self-Attention Let's compare architectures for mapping a sequence of n tokens to another one of equal length, where each input or output token is represented by a d-dimensional vector. Specifically, we will consider CNNs, RNNs, and self-attention. We will compare their computational complexity, sequential operations, and maximum path lengths. Note that sequential operations prevent parallel computation, while a shorter path between any combination of sequence positions makes it easier to learn long-range dependencies within the sequence . Comparing CNN (padding tokens are omitted), RNN, and self-attention architectures. Let's regard any text sequence as a "one-dimensional image". Similarly, one-dimensional CNNs can process local features such as n-grams in text. Given a sequence of length n, consider a convolutional layer whose kernel size is k, and whose numbers of input and output channels are both d. The computational complexity of the convolutional layer is . As shows, CNNs are hierarchical, so there are sequential operations and the maximum path length is . For example, and are within the receptive field of a two-layer CNN with kernel size 3 in . When updating the hidden state of RNNs, multiplication of the weight matrix and the d-dimensional hidden state has a computational complexity of . Since the sequence length is n, the computational complexity of the recurrent layer is . According to , there are sequential operations that cannot be parallelized and the maximum path length is also . In self-attention, the queries, keys, and values are all matrices. Consider the scaled dot product attention in , where an matrix is multiplied by a matrix, then the output matrix is multiplied by an matrix. As a result, the self-attention has a computational complexity. As we can see from , each token is directly connected to any other token via self-attention. Therefore, computation can be parallel with sequential operations and the maximum path length is also . All in all, both CNNs and self-attention enjoy parallel computation and self-attention has the shortest maximum path length. However, the quadratic computational complexity with respect to the sequence length makes self-attention prohibitively slow for very long sequences. ## Positional Encoding Unlike RNNs, which recurrently process tokens of a sequence one-by-one, self-attention ditches sequential operations in favor of parallel computation. Note that self-attention by itself does not preserve the order of the sequence. What do we do if it really matters that the model knows in which order the input sequence arrived? The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token. These inputs are called positional encodings, and they can either be learned or fixed a priori. We now describe a simple scheme for fixed positional encodings based on sine and cosine functions . Suppose that the input representation contains the d-dimensional embeddings for n tokens of a sequence. The positional encoding outputs using a positional embedding matrix of the same shape, whose element on the row and the or the column is At first glance, this trigonometric function design looks weird. Before we give explanations of this design, let's first implement it in the following PositionalEncoding class. In the positional embedding matrix , rows correspond to positions within a sequence and columns represent different positional encoding dimensions. In the example below, we can see that the and the columns of the positional embedding matrix have a higher frequency than the and the columns. The offset between the and the (same for the and the ) columns is due to the alternation of sine and cosine functions. ### Absolute Positional Information To see how the monotonically decreased frequency along the encoding dimension relates to absolute positional information, let's print out the binary representations of . As we can see, the lowest bit, the second-lowest bit, and the third-lowest bit alternate on every number, every two numbers, and every four numbers, respectively. In binary representations, a higher bit has a lower frequency than a lower bit. Similarly, as demonstrated in the heat map below, the positional encoding decreases frequencies along the encoding dimension by using trigonometric functions. Since the outputs are float numbers, such continuous representations are more space-efficient than binary representations. ### Relative Positional Information Besides capturing absolute positional information, the above positional encoding also allows a model to easily learn to attend by relative positions. This is because for any fixed position offset , the positional encoding at position can be represented by a linear projection of that at position i. This projection can be explained mathematically. Denoting , any pair of in can be linearly projected to for any fixed offset : where the projection matrix does not depend on any position index i. ## Summary In self-attention, the queries, keys, and values all come from the same place. Both CNNs and self-attention enjoy parallel computation and self-attention has the shortest maximum path length. However, the quadratic computational complexity with respect to the sequence length makes self-attention prohibitively slow for very long sequences. To use the sequence order information, we can inject absolute or relative positional information by adding positional encoding to the input representations. ## Exercises 1. Suppose that we design a deep architecture to represent a sequence by stacking self-attention layers with positional encoding. What could the possible issues be? 1. Can you design a learnable positional encoding method? 1. Can we assign different learned embeddings according to different offsets between queries and keys that are compared in self-attention? Hint: you may refer to relative position embeddings . :begin_tab:mxnet Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:pytorch Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:tensorflow Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:jax Discussions :end_tab:

sources.title: Dive into Deep Learning — 4.1 Softmax Regression
sources.author: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola
sources.text: # Softmax Regression In , we introduced linear regression, working through implementations from scratch in and again using high-level APIs of a deep learning framework in to do the heavy lifting. Regression is the hammer we reach for when we want to answer how much? or how many? questions. If you want to predict the number of dollars (price) at which a house will be sold, or the number of wins a baseball team might have, or the number of days that a patient will remain hospitalized before being discharged, then you are probably looking for a regression model. However, even within regression models, there are important distinctions. For instance, the price of a house will never be negative and changes might often be relative to its baseline price. As such, it might be more effective to regress on the logarithm of the price. Likewise, the number of days a patient spends in hospital is a discrete nonnegative random variable. As such, least mean squares might not be an ideal approach either. This sort of time-to-event modeling comes with a host of other complications that are dealt with in a specialized subfield called survival modeling. The point here is not to overwhelm you but just to let you know that there is a lot more to estimation than simply minimizing squared errors. And more broadly, there is a lot more to supervised learning than regression. In this section, we focus on classification problems where we put aside how much? questions and instead focus on which category? questions. Does this email belong in the spam folder or the inbox? Is this customer more likely to sign up or not to sign up for a subscription service? Does this image depict a donkey, a dog, a cat, or a rooster? Which movie is Aston most likely to watch next? Which section of the book are you going to read next? Colloquially, machine learning practitioners overload the word classification to describe two subtly different problems: (i) those where we are interested only in hard assignments of examples to categories (classes); and (ii) those where we wish to make soft assignments, i.e., to assess the probability that each category applies. The distinction tends to get blurred, in part, because often, even when we only care about hard assignments, we still use models that make soft assignments. Even more, there are cases where more than one label might be true. For instance, a news article might simultaneously cover the topics of entertainment, business, and space flight, but not the topics of medicine or sports. Thus, categorizing it into one of the above categories on their own would not be very useful. This problem is commonly known as multi-label classification. See for an overview and for an effective algorithm when tagging images. ## Classification To get our feet wet, let's start with a simple image classification problem. Here, each input consists of a grayscale image. We can represent each pixel value with a single scalar, giving us four features . Further, let's assume that each image belongs to one among the categories "cat", "chicken", and "dog". Next, we have to choose how to represent the labels. We have two obvious choices. Perhaps the most natural impulse would be to choose , where the integers represent respectively. This is a great way of storing such information on a computer. If the categories had some natural ordering among them, say if we were trying to predict , then it might even make sense to cast this as an ordinal regression problem and keep the labels in this format. See for an overview of different types of ranking loss functions and for a Bayesian approach that addresses responses with more than one mode. In general, classification problems do not come with natural orderings among the classes. Fortunately, statisticians long ago invented a simple way to represent categorical data: the one-hot encoding. A one-hot encoding is a vector with as many components as we have categories. The component corresponding to a particular instance's category is set to 1 and all other components are set to 0. In our case, a label y would be a three-dimensional vector, with corresponding to "cat", to "chicken", and to "dog": ### Linear Model In order to estimate the conditional probabilities associated with all the possible classes, we need a model with multiple outputs, one per class. To address classification with linear models, we will need as many affine functions as we have outputs. Strictly speaking, we only need one fewer, since the final category has to be the difference between 1 and the sum of the other categories, but for reasons of symmetry we use a slightly redundant parametrization. Each output corresponds to its own affine function. In our case, since we have 4 features and 3 possible output categories, we need 12 scalars to represent the weights (w with subscripts), and 3 scalars to represent the biases (b with subscripts). This yields: The corresponding neural network diagram is shown in . Just as in linear regression, we use a single-layer neural network. And since the calculation of each output, , and , depends on every input, , , , and , the output layer can also be described as a fully connected layer. Softmax regression is a single-layer neural network. For a more concise notation we use vectors and matrices: is much better suited for mathematics and code. Note that we have gathered all of our weights into a matrix and all biases in a vector. ### The Softmax Assuming a suitable loss function, we could try, directly, to minimize the difference between and the labels . While it turns out that treating classification as a vector-valued regression problem works surprisingly well, it is nonetheless unsatisfactory in the following ways: There is no guarantee that the outputs sum up to 1 in the way we expect probabilities to behave. There is no guarantee that the outputs are even nonnegative, even if their outputs sum up to 1, or that they do not exceed 1. Both aspects render the estimation problem difficult to solve and the solution very brittle to outliers.
